paper_metadata.title: Overcoming public resistance to carbon taxes
paper_metadata.authors: Stefano Carattini; Maria Carvalho; Sam Fankhauser
paper_metadata.geographic_scope: International; evidence from Europe, North America, Australia, and Switzerland.
paper_metadata.empirical_scope_or_sample: Narrative review of surveys, discrete-choice experiments, focus groups, referenda, field observations, and lab experiments on carbon, fuel, gasoline, and related energy taxes.
paper_metadata.carbon_tax_relevance_summary: Reviews direct evidence on public support, opposition, and willingness to pay for carbon and fuel taxes, with emphasis on tax rates, revenue recycling, earmarking, fairness, trust, and communication.

determinants.definition: Higher carbon or fuel tax rates, or larger personal cost burdens, reduce acceptability.
determinants.mechanism: Higher private costs make the policy feel more expensive and coercive, so voters focus on immediate losses rather than environmental gains.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Narrative review of carbon, fuel, gasoline, and energy taxes in Europe and North America.
determinants.outcome_variable: public support/acceptability of carbon or fuel tax
determinants.evidence_quote: acceptability of a tax proposal decreases with the personal cost it would impose
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Acceptability fell almost linearly as tax rates rose.

determinants.definition: Perceived personal cost of the tax reduces support.
determinants.mechanism: People weigh immediate household burden more heavily than abstract environmental benefits.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax and fuel tax acceptability studies reviewed in Sections 2–3.
determinants.evidence_quote: People's attitudes to carbon taxes appear to be influenced more by the direct personal cost of the measure

determinants.definition: Experience with a tax through trials or implementation increases acceptability.
determinants.mechanism: People update beliefs after seeing actual costs and benefits, including reduced congestion, emissions, or waste.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Trial and post-implementation evidence from carbon, fuel, congestion, and waste pricing studies summarized in Section 3.2.
determinants.outcome_variable: support/acceptance of tax after trial or implementation
determinants.evidence_quote: voter aversion may abate once a policy is implemented
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Support rose after trial periods and after policy implementation.

determinants.definition: Dedicating carbon tax revenues to environmental spending increases support.
determinants.mechanism: Earmarking signals effectiveness and reduces distrust that revenues will disappear into general government budgets.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon tax revenue recycling and earmarking evidence in Section 4.1 and Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: public acceptance for a carbon tax is higher if the use of proceeds is clearly specified
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Earmarking often ranked above other recycling options.

determinants.definition: Returning revenues evenly or targeting low-income households increases support.
determinants.mechanism: Redistribution offsets regressivity and makes the tax appear fairer and less burdensome for vulnerable households.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Revenue recycling designs discussed in Section 4.2 and Table 1.
determinants.outcome_variable: support for carbon tax revenue recycling design
determinants.evidence_quote: carbon taxes with lump-sum transfers tend overall to be progressive
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Progressive recycling outperformed neutral or regressive designs.

determinants.definition: Progressive rates or threshold exemptions raise acceptability relative to flat, regressive designs.
determinants.mechanism: Visible progressivity reduces fairness concerns by shifting more burden toward higher users or higher-income households.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Choice experiments on carbon and fuel tax design in Norway, Sweden, and Turkey.
determinants.evidence_quote: respondents preferred progressive tax rates to address distributional concerns related with the tax burden on low-income households
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Progressive designs performed better than neutral or regressive designs.

determinants.definition: Using carbon tax revenue to cut other taxes is less popular than earmarking or redistribution.
determinants.mechanism: Voters often see the environmental tax and the tax cut as separate issues and distrust whether the shift will actually occur.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Revenue-neutral carbon/fuel tax designs reviewed in Section 4.3 and Appendix A.
determinants.outcome_variable: support for revenue-neutral carbon/fuel tax design
determinants.evidence_quote: the least popular redistribution strategy among the public
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Revenue-neutral designs were generally least preferred.

determinants.definition: Greater trust in government or politicians raises support for carbon tax increases.
determinants.mechanism: Trust reduces suspicion that the tax is a revenue grab and increases credibility of promised recycling or environmental outcomes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax increase and fuel-tax reform studies in Sweden, Germany, and Denmark.
determinants.evidence_quote: trust in politicians is the most significant factor to support an increase in carbon tax rates
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Trust mattered more than self-interest in explaining resistance.

determinants.definition: Belief that the tax reduces emissions increases support.
determinants.mechanism: When people believe the tax changes behavior and lowers emissions, they are less likely to see it as pointless or unfair.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon and fuel tax studies summarized in Sections 2–5 and Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: increased confidence in the effectiveness of the carbon tax to reduce emissions increases support for raising the carbon tax
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Perceived effectiveness was a key driver of higher support.

determinants.definition: Providing clear information about costs, benefits, and revenue use increases support.
determinants.mechanism: Information corrects misperceptions about effectiveness, distributional impacts, and what households receive back from the tax.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Communication and information strategies for carbon-tax design and revenue recycling in Sections 5.4 and Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: Providing this information reduced voters' doubt about the effectiveness of carbon taxes
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Information reduced demand for earmarking and raised acceptance of progressive recycling.

determinants.definition: Greater climate concern or environmental awareness increases support.
determinants.mechanism: People who care more about climate change are less focused on short-run price costs and more receptive to mitigation instruments.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Carbon-tax and energy-tax choice experiments in Sweden, Turkey, and Switzerland.
determinants.outcome_variable: support for carbon tax / tax design
determinants.evidence_quote: Respondents with high environmental awareness were more supportive of a carbon tax
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Low climate concern increased sensitivity to tax rates.

determinants.definition: Left/green or egalitarian-communitarian worldviews increase support; hierarchical-individualistic worldviews reduce it.
determinants.mechanism: Values shape whether taxes are seen as legitimate collective action or coercive intervention, especially when redistribution is involved.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum and experimental evidence on worldviews summarized in Appendix A and Section 2.
determinants.evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had leftist or green affinities
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Hierarchical/individualistic respondents were more averse to policy intervention.
determinants.notes: Direction is mixed because different value dimensions predict opposite responses.

determinants.definition: Higher education is associated with greater acceptance of energy taxes.
determinants.mechanism: More educated respondents may better understand tax design, recycling, and environmental benefits.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... higher education levels
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Education increased probability of a yes vote.

determinants.definition: Urban residents are more accepting of energy taxes than non-urban residents.
determinants.mechanism: Urban residents may face lower dependence on driving and see greater local environmental benefits or alternatives.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... lived in cities
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: City residents were more likely to support the tax.

determinants.definition: Car owners are less accepting of fuel and energy taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Vehicle owners expect greater direct fuel-cost exposure and therefore oppose fuel-related taxes more strongly.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Fuel and energy tax survey and referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... did not own cars

determinants.definition: Younger respondents are more accepting of energy taxes than older respondents.
determinants.mechanism: Younger respondents may be more favorable to policy change or more concerned about future climate impacts.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Swiss energy-tax referendum evidence summarized in Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: Respondents were more accepting of energy taxes if they had ... were younger than 60 years old

determinants.definition: Higher-income households are more likely to support carbon taxes.
determinants.mechanism: Higher income reduces exposure to the tax burden and increases ability to absorb price changes.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: US national carbon-tax survey summarized in Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: a US$10,000 increase in a household's annual income increased the likelihood of support by 1 percentage point
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Income had a positive marginal effect on support.

determinants.definition: Fear of competitiveness losses, job losses, or factory closures reduces support.
determinants.mechanism: People worry that firms or workers exposed to fuel or carbon costs will lose jobs or relocate, hurting the wider economy.
determinants.exact_tax_policy_context: Fuel and energy tax opposition studies in Switzerland, Ireland, and Australia summarized in Sections 2–3 and Appendix A.
determinants.evidence_quote: Loss of competitiveness and jobs and closure of factories were further concerns
determinants.statistical_or_result_detail: Competitiveness concerns helped explain rejection in several cases.

exclusions_or_uncertainties.reason: Mentioned mainly in relation to broader environmental policies and congestion charging; not retained because it was not clearly specific to carbon or fuel taxes.
The review notes tax/fee framing in lab studies, but tax-specific carbon or fuel-tax evidence was not sufficiently explicit for a separate determinant.
